one_line_takeaway: A theory-forward survey arguing that LLM hallucination is a Godel/Turing-inevitable property of LLMs-as-formal-systems, with a formal three-type taxonomy and a broad (but non-quantitative) catalog of detection and mitigation methods.
key_finding: The survey's headline thesis is that LLM hallucination is fundamentally UNAVOIDABLE -- because a sufficiently expressive LLM viewed as a formal system inherits Godel/Turing undecidability limits -- and therefore current mitigations are structurally bounded rather than eventually eliminable. Secondary synthesized takeaways: post-training (SFT/RLHF/PPO) tends to improve faithfulness but degrade confidence calibration, inducing overconfident factual hallucinations; fine-tuning to inject new knowledge can linearly increase hallucination because models forget faster than they acquire (attributed to Gekhman et al.); and detection/mitigation reduce to managing (not removing) this residual.
core_contribution: A survey of hallucination in LLMs whose central novel claim (vs. prior hallucination surveys) is a unified THEORETICAL framing: it argues hallucinations are mathematically inevitable by appealing to Godel's incompleteness theorems and Turing's halting problem, and it packages this with (1) a set-theoretic formal definition of hallucination (a response LLM(q) is a hallucination iff (q, LLM(q)) not in S, the set of valid query-response pairs), (2) a three-way taxonomy of hallucination types (factual, faithfulness, and logical-inconsistency), each given a formal predicate (HF, HFh, HL) plus a 'procedural hallucination' HP (right answer via unsupported reasoning), and (3) a 'task-aware' organization of evaluation, detection, and mitigation methods. The bulk of the paper is a literature review of detection (white-box: hidden-layer/logit/activation methods, semantic entropy; black-box: consistency, verbalized confidence, auxiliary-model/tool methods) and mitigation (abstention/calibration, RAG, knowledge graphs, prompt engineering/CoT, reflection, fine-tuning/LoRA/AlphaEdit, structural and decoding optimization). Table I positions this survey against seven prior surveys on coverage dimensions (formalization, empirical causes, taxonomy, detection, evaluation, mitigation, datasets, limitations, future directions).
threat_model: N/A. This is not an adversarial-security paper. Hallucination is framed as an intrinsic reliability/trustworthiness failure of LLMs (with downstream harm cited in finance, law, healthcare, and education), not as the product of an attacker. There is no adversary, attack surface, or defense-vs-attacker analysis; the causes discussed (data staleness/contamination as incidental noise, attention 'short-sightedness', decoding path-blindness, post-training miscalibration, and Godelian undecidability) are inherent-model limitations rather than exploited vulnerabilities. Forcing a threat model onto this paper would misrepresent it.
limitations: (1) The 'inevitability' proofs are informal analogies -- mapping an autoregressive next-token model onto a Turing machine / consistent formal arithmetic is asserted, not established, so the Godel/halting claims are suggestive rather than binding. (2) The survey reports NO quantitative comparison of the detection/mitigation methods it catalogs -- Table I only tallies which topics each prior survey covers (checkmarks), not any performance numbers -- so readers get no empirical sense of which methods work better. (3) The taxonomy boundaries are admittedly fuzzy (the paper shows factual/faithfulness/logical errors co-occurring in one example). (4) The authors themselves flag open problems: lack of universal, leakage-resistant evaluation, and the gap between benchmark and real-world hallucination. (5) Some illustrative numbers (e.g., 90% accuracy on a 90/10 imbalanced set, '10 trillion tokens' training corpora) are pedagogical, not measured results. (6) Minor sloppiness in the text (author-affiliation inconsistencies, a typo'd claim that the LLM both does and does not decode the phoenix riddle) slightly undercuts polish.

datasets_used: None used experimentally -- this is a survey with no experiments of its own.

Benchmarks/datasets REVIEWED (cited third-party, not run by the authors): HaluEval, DEFAN, HaluQuestQA, DiSQ, FaithDial, IFEval, TriviaQA, HotpotQA, plus unanswerable-math and long-form-QA benchmarks.
quantitative_results: No empirical results of the authors' own; the survey reports zero measured performance numbers.

Table I is a qualitative coverage matrix (checkmark/cross) comparing this survey to 7 prior surveys (Tonmo et al., Rawte et al. x2, Zhang et al., Huang et al., Bai et al., Jiang et al.) across 10 dimensions -- not experimental metrics.

Only illustrative figures appear: e.g., a toy 90/10 class-imbalance example yielding 90% accuracy, and a stated '>10 trillion tokens' scale for contemporary LLM training corpora (attributed to Kaplan et al.).
baselines_compared: None empirically. The paper's only 'comparison' is Table I's topical-coverage self-positioning against seven prior hallucination surveys; no method-vs-method or model-vs-model baseline experiments are conducted.
